message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in current code (6 passes hardened). confirmed=true only if real, reproducible, worth fixing (not speculative/by-design).
J-005: T3 transient error-classification tests are non-discriminating: assert base ReasoningError, so a transient->permanent/rate-limit misclassification still passes
File: tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py:143-146, 168-171, 202-206
VERIFIED. The T3 drain classifier's purpose is to map an SDK error to the CORRECT subtype because the retry executor branches on exact subtype (PermanentReasoningError => no retry; RateLimitError => long backpressure; base ReasoningError => fast backoff). Both subtypes subclass ReasoningError. Three drain tests assert classification only via pytest.raises(ReasoningError) (the base class): test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (145), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500, 170), test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (205). Since pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even if the code misclassifies — e.g. mapping a generic 500 to PermanentReasoningError (don't retry) or RateLimitError still satisfies the assertion. The transient-vs-permanent distinction these tests claim to guard is exactly what is NOT asserted. By contrast, the same file's specific tests correctly pin subtypes (test_t3_auth_error_maps_to_permanent:164 -> PermanentReasoningError; test_t3_overloaded_529_maps_to_ratelimit:182 -> RateLimitError), confirming an isolated weakness, not a convention.
PoC: In seam.py replace the generic fall-through `raise ReasoningError(...)` with `raise PermanentReasoningError(...)` (a real misclassification: a transient 500 becomes non-retryable). Re-run test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient — it still PASSES because PermanentReasoningError subclasses ReasoningError; the test fails to detect the 500 is no longer retried.
Fix: Make the transient assertions discriminating: capture the exception and assert it is exactly the base/transient type and not the specific subtypes, e.g. `with pytest.raises(ReasoningError) as ei: _run(); assert type(ei.value) is ReasoningError; assert not isinstance(ei.value, (PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError))` (or introduce a dedicated TransientReasoningError and assert it). Apply to all three tests so a transient->permanent/rate-limit regression fails every run.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The finding is precisely accurate about the test code. The three transient tests (143-146, 168-171, 202-206) assert only `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)`, the base class, which catches the subtypes `PermanentReasoningError` and `RateLimitError`. Let me verify the class hierarchy and the actual classification code to confirm the PoC claim is reproducible.

Confirmed: both subtypes subclass `ReasoningError`. So `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)` catches them all. Let me check the classification fall-through and the retry executor branching to confirm the consequence is real.

585	    ``deadline`` plus the full retry budget of per-call timeouts.
586	    """
587	    transient = 0
588	    rate = 0
589	    attempted = False
590	    while True:
591	        # Gate retry ENTRY on the deadline (#F-022): the first attempt always runs, but a retry is
592	        # not started once the deadline is past — otherwise the per-attempt timeouts could stack
593	        # well beyond the overall budget even though the backoff sleeps were bounded.
594	        if attempted and deadline is not None and time.monotonic() >= deadline:
595	            raise ReasoningError("reasoning deadline exceeded before retry")
596	        attempted = True
597	        try:
598	            return _attempt(request)
599	        except PermanentReasoningError:
600	            raise
601	        except RateLimitError:
602	            if rate >= RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES:
603	                raise
604	            _sleep_bounded(RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF * (rate + 1), deadline)
605	            rate += 1
606	        except ReasoningError:
607	            if transient >= MAX_RETRIES:
608	                raise
609	            _sleep_bounded(BACKOFF_BASE**transient, deadline)

content.text:
The branching is confirmed: `PermanentReasoningError` => no retry (re-raise), `RateLimitError` => long backpressure (RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF), base `ReasoningError` => fast backoff (BACKOFF_BASE). The retry behavior depends entirely on the exact subtype.

The PoC is reproducible: if line 498 (`raise ReasoningError(...status=500...)`) were changed to `PermanentReasoningError`, a transient 500 would no longer be retried, but `test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient` would still pass because it only asserts `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)` which catches the subclass.

This is a real test weakness. The three transient tests do not discriminate between the base type and its subtypes, so a transient→permanent or transient→rate-limit misclassification regression would go undetected. The other tests in the same file correctly pin exact subtypes, proving this is an isolated gap, not a deliberate convention.

Assessment: this is a true production-readiness problem — a test-quality/regression-detection gap that defeats the exact purpose those tests claim to guard. Severity is medium: it's a test-only weakness (no current incorrect production behavior), but it leaves the retry-classification logic — which directly affects whether transient frontier errors are retried vs. permanently dropped — without a guard against future regressions.

input.reasoning:
Confirmed against current code. In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py both PermanentReasoningError (line 70) and RateLimitError (line 74) subclass ReasoningError (line 66). The retry executor branches on exact subtype: PermanentReasoningError => re-raise/no retry (line 599-600), RateLimitError => long backpressure RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF (601-605), base ReasoningError => fast BACKOFF_BASE backoff (606-609). So retry behavior depends entirely on which subtype the classifier raises.

The three transient tests in tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py — test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (145), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (170), test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (205) — assert only pytest.raises(ReasoningError), the base class. Since pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even if the code misclassifies a transient error as PermanentReasoningError or RateLimitError. The PoC is reproducible: changing line 498 (raise ReasoningError ... status=500) to PermanentReasoningError makes a transient 500 non-retryable, yet test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient still passes. The transient-vs-permanent distinction the tests claim to guard is exactly what is not asserted.

This is an isolated weakness, not a convention: sibling tests correctly pin exact subtypes (test_t3_auth_error_maps_to_permanent:164 -> PermanentReasoningError; test_t3_overloaded_529_maps_to_ratelimit:184 -> RateLimitError; test_t3_rate_limit_event_rejected_maps_to_ratelimit:151).

Severity medium rather than high: no current production misbehavior — it is a test-quality/regression-detection gap. But it leaves the retry-classification logic (which determines whether transient frontier errors are retried or permanently dropped) unguarded against a real, plausible regression. Fix is the proposed discriminating assertion (capture exc and assert type is exactly ReasoningError and not isinstance of the subtypes) or a dedicated TransientReasoningError. Worth fixing.

content.text: Confirmed J-005 as a true, medium-severity test-quality problem. The three transient tests at tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py:143-146, 168-171, 202-206 assert only the base `ReasoningError`, which catches its `PermanentReasoningError` and `RateLimitError` subclasses, so a transient→permanent/rate-limit misclassification regression in src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py would pass silently despite the retry executor (seam.py:599-609) branching on exact subtype.
